sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 54, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has two vertical "channels" in the black shape - left at col 21, right at col 36. LEFT/RIGHT moves both squares horizontally by 5 when at same row. UP/DOWN moves squares through their channels by 5 rows, but only if they're in the correct column. Current: left at (41, 21), right at (46, 36). The right square is in the right channel and needs to go UP into the orange region.
- Recent findings: Blue squares moved from row 51 to row 46 (up 5 rows again). They're moving up consistently through the black shape's "legs".
- Plan: Try UP to move the right square from (46, 36) to (41, 36) into the orange region.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 53 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT didn't change anything visible. The squares are still at the same positions. This confirms that RIGHT only works when both squares are at the same row. Currently they're at different rows (41 and 46).
Let me think about what I know:
- UP works when squares are at rows 46 or 51, moving them up by 5
- DOWN moves squares down by 5
- LEFT/RIGHT move squares horizontally by 5, but only when at the same row
- Current positions: left at (41, 21), right at (46, 36)
To move them horizontally, I need to align them first. Let me try DOWN to move the left square from row 41 to row 46, then both would be at row 46 and I could try RIGHT.
Wait, but the left square is at row 41. If I do DOWN, would it move from 41 to 46? Let me check the pattern:
- UP moves from 46 to 41 (up 5)
- So DOWN should move from 41 to 46 (down 5)
But wait, the left square is in the left channel at col 21. The right square is in the right channel at col 36. They're in different columns, so they might not both move when I press DOWN.
Actually, looking at the history more carefully:
- When both were at (46, 21) and (46, 36), UP moved both to (41, 21) and (41, 36)
- When left was at (41, 21) and right at (41, 36), DOWN moved both to (46, 21) and (46, 36)
- When left was at (46, 21) and right at (51, 36), UP moved both to (41, 21) and (46, 36)
So it seems like UP/DOWN moves BOTH squares regardless of their row position, as long as they're in valid channel positions.
Let me try DOWN to move the left square from 41 to 46, which should also move the right square from 46 to 51.
Actually wait - let me reconsider. When both were at row 46, UP moved them to row 41. When left was at 41 and right at 46, UP moved left to stay at 41 (already at top) and right to 41... no wait, that's not right.
Let me re-examine:
- Step 50: positions were (46, 21) and (51, 36)
- After UP at step 50: (41, 21) and (46, 36)
So UP moved left from 46→41 and right from 51→46. Both moved up by 5.
Now current is (41, 21) and (46, 36). If I do DOWN:
- Left: 41→46
- Right: 46→51
Then both would be at different rows again. But then I could try RIGHT... but RIGHT requires same row.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe I need to get both squares to the same row first, then move them horizontally.
To get both to the same row:
- Current: left at 41, right at 46
- If I do DOWN: left goes to 46, right goes to 51 → still different
- If I do UP: left stays at 41 (can't go higher?), right goes to 41 → both at 41!
Wait, let me check if UP works when left is already at 41. From the history, when left was at 41 and right at 46, UP was attempted but didn't work (the board didn't change).
So the rule seems to be: UP only works if at least one square can move up. If both are already at the top of their channel, UP doesn't work.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the board more carefully to understand what the goal might be.
Actually, let me just try DOWN to move both down, then see if I can get them aligned.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: RIGHT did nothing - squares at different rows can't move horizontally together. Let me try DOWN to shift both down, then explore alignment.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN to move both squares down
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
